title: Cleveland (county)

infobox.value: Two-tier - upper-tier county council with four lower-tier non-metropolitan borough councils

1. [Hartlepool](/wiki/Borough_of_Hartlepool "Borough of Hartlepool") 2. [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Borough_of_Stockton-on-Tees "Borough of Stockton-on-Tees") 3. [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Borough_of_Middlesbrough "Borough of Middlesbrough") 4. [Langbaurgh-on-Tees](/wiki/Redcar_and_Cleveland "Redcar and Cleveland")

was a [non-metropolitan county](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county") in [North East England](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England") between 1974 and 1996. It was a two-tier county and had four boroughs: [Hartlepool](/wiki/Borough_of_Hartlepool "Borough of Hartlepool"), [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Borough_of_Stockton-on-Tees "Borough of Stockton-on-Tees"), [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Borough_of_Middlesbrough "Borough of Middlesbrough") and [Langbaurgh-on-Tees](/wiki/Redcar_and_Cleveland "Redcar and Cleveland"). The [county town](/wiki/County_town "County town") was [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough "Middlesbrough"), where [Cleveland County Council](/wiki/Cleveland_County_Council "Cleveland County Council") met. The county was named after the historic area of [Cleveland, Yorkshire](/wiki/Cleveland,_Yorkshire "Cleveland, Yorkshire"). Its area is now split between the [ceremonial counties](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") of [North Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Yorkshire "North Yorkshire") and [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), but remains wholly within the North East England region.

[*Local government in England and Wales: A Guide to the New System*](https://books.google.com/books?id=ISBN0117508470). London: [HMSO](/wiki/HMSO "HMSO"). 1974. p. 37. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-11-750847-0](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-11-750847-0 "Special:BookSources/0-11-750847-0").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Former county of North East England

This article is about the former county in England. For other uses, see [Cleveland County (disambiguation)](/wiki/Cleveland_County_(disambiguation) "Cleveland County (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Cleveland, Yorkshire](/wiki/Cleveland,_Yorkshire "Cleveland, Yorkshire").

**Cleveland** was a [non-metropolitan county](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county") in [North East England](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England") between 1974 and 1996. It was a two-tier county and had four boroughs: [Hartlepool](/wiki/Borough_of_Hartlepool "Borough of Hartlepool"), [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Borough_of_Stockton-on-Tees "Borough of Stockton-on-Tees"), [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Borough_of_Middlesbrough "Borough of Middlesbrough") and [Langbaurgh-on-Tees](/wiki/Redcar_and_Cleveland "Redcar and Cleveland"). The [county town](/wiki/County_town "County town") was [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough "Middlesbrough"), where [Cleveland County Council](/wiki/Cleveland_County_Council "Cleveland County Council") met. The county was named after the historic area of [Cleveland, Yorkshire](/wiki/Cleveland,_Yorkshire "Cleveland, Yorkshire"). Its area is now split between the [ceremonial counties](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") of [North Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Yorkshire "North Yorkshire") and [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), but remains wholly within the North East England region.

The county was abolished in 1996, with its constituent boroughs becoming [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England"). Hartlepool and the part of Stockton-on-Tees north of the River Tees became part of County Durham, and the remainder became part of North Yorkshire. Some public bodies continue to cover the area of the former county, such as [Cleveland Police](/wiki/Cleveland_Police "Cleveland Police") and [Cleveland Fire Brigade](/wiki/Cleveland_Fire_Brigade "Cleveland Fire Brigade"). Cleveland bordered County Durham to the north and North Yorkshire to the south, with a coastline on the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the east. It had a total area of 225 square miles (583 km2).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Middlesbrough_Transporter_Bridge_%28geograph_6425250%29.jpg/250px-Middlesbrough_Transporter_Bridge_%28geograph_6425250%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Middlesbrough_Transporter_Bridge_(geograph_6425250).jpg)

The [Tees Transporter Bridge](/wiki/Tees_Transporter_Bridge "Tees Transporter Bridge"), in 1980

## Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_(county)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Formation")]

A Bill as originally presented in November 1971 that intended the administrative county to have been an extended form of the then present [County Borough of Teesside](/wiki/County_Borough_of_Teesside "County Borough of Teesside"), an independent district in the [North Riding](/wiki/North_Riding "North Riding") from 1968 to 1974. On 1 April 1974, by the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"), most of the then [Cleveland constituency](/wiki/Cleveland_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Cleveland (UK Parliament constituency)") and Hartlepool were incorporated as the Cleveland [non-metropolitan county](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Proposed abolition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_(county)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Proposed abolition")]

Local government reorganisation, recommended by the [Banham Review](/wiki/Banham_Review "Banham Review") and accepted by the government, meant that each district borough be re-organised into separate unitary authorities with the Tees be re-established as a ceremonial border between [North Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Yorkshire "North Yorkshire") and [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-opsi1-5) The county district boroughs of Cleveland were re-organised into [Hartlepool](/wiki/Borough_of_Hartlepool "Borough of Hartlepool"), [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Borough_of_Stockton-on-Tees "Borough of Stockton-on-Tees"), [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Borough_of_Middlesbrough "Borough of Middlesbrough") and [Redcar and Cleveland](/wiki/Redcar_and_Cleveland "Redcar and Cleveland"). The reorganisation meant that Stockton-on-Tees became the only local authority in England to be split between two counties.

This split was contested by Cleveland [County Council](/wiki/County_Council "County Council"), which applied for [judicial review](/wiki/Judicial_review "Judicial review") over the decision. According to the Minister, [David Curry](/wiki/David_Curry "David Curry"), in the Commons debate on the order on 11 January 1995, this caused a delay from 1 April 1995 as the reorganisation date to 1 April 1996.

## Abolition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_(county)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Abolition")]

*The Cleveland (Structural Change) Order 1995* abolished the County Council (to take place on 1 April 1996) and transferred its powers to the district councils, though it did not abolish Cleveland itself. It also renamed Langbaurgh-on-Tees as Redcar and Cleveland.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

*The Cleveland (Further Provision) Order 1995* abolished the county of Cleveland altogether, also from 1 April 1996, creating in its place four counties corresponding to the four boroughs. However, the requirement for counties to have a council was removed for these four counties in particular.[[5]](#cite_note-opsi1-5) Further regulations placed each district within the [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") of [Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham") or [North Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Yorkshire "North Yorkshire"), also splitting Stockton-on-Tees along the Tees.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Town twinning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_(county)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Town twinning")]

See also: [List of twin towns and sister cities in the United Kingdom](/wiki/List_of_twin_towns_and_sister_cities_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of twin towns and sister cities in the United Kingdom")

Cleveland, as a conurbation of settlements, was [twinned](/wiki/Twin_towns_and_sister_cities "Twin towns and sister cities") with:

* [![United States](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a4/Flag_of_the_United_States.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_United_States.svg.png)](/wiki/United_States "United States") [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), Ohio, United States[[9]](#cite_note-Cleveland_sisters-9)

## Statistical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_(county)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Statistical")]

[NUTS statistical regions of the United Kingdom](/wiki/NUTS_statistical_regions_of_the_United_Kingdom "NUTS statistical regions of the United Kingdom") were also introduced in 1974. This caused South [Humberside](/wiki/Humberside "Humberside") to be put with the reformed counties using the name Yorkshire, to form [Yorkshire and Humber](/wiki/Yorkshire_and_Humber "Yorkshire and Humber"). South Tees came under [North East of England](/wiki/North_East_of_England "North East of England") region.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
